title: 2025/09/10 - SSC
displayTitle: 2025-09-10 - Social Services and Community Committee - Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, Oranga Tamariki: Inquiry into procurement and contract management
topic: Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, Oranga Tamariki: Inquiry into procurement and contract management
description: Wednesday, 10 September 2025 | Social Services and Community Committee Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, Oranga Tamariki: Inquiry into procurement and contract management Hearing of Evidence Independent Children's Monitor–Aroturuki Tamariki - Arran Jones, Chief Executive Mana Mokopuna–Children's Commissioner - Dr Claire Achmad, Children’s Commissioner
descriptionSummary: Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, Oranga Tamariki: Inquiry into procurement and contract management. Independent Children's Monitor–Aroturuki Tamariki. Arran Jones, Chief Executive

chunks.text: Well, good morning and welcome to the Social Services Community Select Committee. Uh, this morning we have the Independence Children's Monitor, the children's Commissioner, uh, with the committee. And, uh, we, we are looking into the report of the controller and auditor general inquiry into procurement contract management. And after, um, hearing some earlier evidence, we're keen to hear from you both.

Uh, so, um, what we might do is just, um, if you wanted to make some opening comments and then we'll open up questions from members that works. Okay. And I here, Mr. Chair, and thank you, uh, for the invitation for us to meet with you today. In my role as children's Commissioner, I want to see all children in ALT and New Zealand flourish to their full potential.

And that includes all children and young people in the orang system, services, and, uh, programs and supports that they receive through this system directly impact on their rights and their wellbeing. My advocacy in this space is informed strongly by the views and lived experience of children and young people in this system.

And by the evidence and information that I received from my oversight of Warga Tamari colleagues, including in Roki, tamari, the Independent Children's Monitor in the Office of the Ombudsman in relation to Oranga Tamari's Contracting and procurement, uh, process and management.

My focus has been squarely on seeking to understand how oranga tamari factors the needs, rights of children into its decision making. I've been focused on advocating for children's rights and needs to be at the heart of every single one of these decisions together with aki.

I have raised concerns around the way that funding decisions were made by Oranga Tamari last year, and the very real impacts that these have had on access to services for children and young people, families and hanau. I want to make a couple of other, um, points beforehand handing to Erin, it's crucial in my view that we must take a view now and into the future that weave stronger safety nets for children in alt in New Zealand because the stakes are simply too high for us not to do so.

I've been vocal about my significant concerns when it comes to child maltreatment in this country, and this continues to be evidenced by our high rate of child homicide in this country, including this year where a number of very, very young children have been killed by homicide. I'm very concerned about the gaps that have been opened up in the safety nets around children.

And I emphasize the need for us to weave these safety nets ever stronger around children when it comes to our country's children and how we make decisions. Evidence matters. It really does. Yes, the system is complex, but a holistic view is required of children's lives and careful decision making that pays attention to evidence of what works and what is needed.

Procurement and contracting processes impacting tamari and their wau must be fair, transparent, and grounded in children's rights and best interests. And when addressing our huge societal problems around poverty, around mental health, housing addiction, and family violence, we do need to take a children's rights approach.

And then the final point I want to make this morning is it is crucial that ordering a tamari acts on the recommendations of the Office of the Auditor General's inquiry report, ensuring that children's rights and needs are kept central in all decision making in relation to its contracting and procurement processes.

And I want to see Ang Tamari ground its future work in this, um, regard in detailed analysis of the impacts on children giving careful consideration to their needs and rights. It is encouraging in my view that Ang Tamari leadership has accepted the report's recommendations and taken steps already to improve governance, contract management and program delivery.

But I really do want to emphasize how important rebuilding of trust is to this process. Only high trust and working in a truly partnered way with the wider children's sector will lead to those strongest safety needs for children. I'll hand to you. Thanks Claire. Um, Koto, um, just a couple of, um, comments from Lee before we can take your questions.

But, um, first I just, um, the auditor General's report certainly reflects the concerns that Claire and I raised, um, in mid 2024 with Ang tam. Um, and we were pleased to see, um, and proceed with this report and the outcome. Um, recently I've heard about progress that Ang Tamari are making in response to that report, and that is positive, um, particularly in terms of recommendations one and two of that report.

Um, notably they've been preparing a strategy for their procurement, um, and they've certainly improved the level of engagement and consultation with the sector. Um, so that I'm hearing that from the sector about the positive improvement they're making. Um, however, the strategy, and I, I did watch the presentation from them to this committee in terms of where they're heading, um, certainly reflects the social sector commissioning principles.

There's nothing new there, it's just good practice and it's certainly something that should have been followed. Um, if it had, we may not have got to where we are. Um, and as Claire mentioned, you know, trust, um, comes down in the elevator, but it goes up a staircase. And so I think Oranga, tamari started walking up those stairs. Um, but there is still a way to go and they must demonstrate their words with actions.

Now, I think the challenge for them though, in looking at their strategy is how do they get ahead of the contract expires? So I think Benicia, when she was here, was talking about an 18 month runway in terms of planning and making decisions around contracts. Um, but when you have contracts that are expiring in December and then again in June next year, how do they get the time and the space to make those proper decisions to get a really good understanding of need and community and then putting the services in place to respond to it.

Um, and so that takes time. And what's needed is a period of stability for ang tamari so they can do that and make those, um, detailed decisions. And I think, um, the last point I'll make is that it must also be remembered that although, um, some contracts were extended through to December this year, others have ended or faced funding cuts.

And we hear about the impact of those cuts through our monitoring in communities. Um, they are broad. Um, they include cuts to transition support, disability services, youth justice intervention programs, counseling and therapy, for example. Um, cutting services not only in terms of their contracted spend, but also restrictions on spending within Ang Tamari makes it difficult for social workers to do their job and to connect whanau and children up with the right services.

This also impacts on their ability to recruit caregivers because the supports aren't always there to support caregivers as well. Um, we hear about Ang Tamari asking for favors from providers, asking them to do more with less. Um, and also there've been delays in payments, but I know that they're addressing that now. I think the learning is, or at least, um, a point I'd just like to make at the end is that some of the cutting of, um, services or reduction in services that operate in the prevention space run counter to an investment approach.

And our recent report on outcomes from Maori shows that the further that tamari he go through the system, the worse their outcomes are. So there's an imperative that we can put services in place to help families in hanana at the stage to prevent the downward costs, not only in economic terms, but also in terms of human terms.

And I think I'll just finish with the one one quote from, um, our monitoring, our recent monitoring from the Bay of Plenty. And this is what happens when you follow a process that doesn't properly consider the needs of children who don't communicate and plan, um, your transition when you're decommissioning services. And it's, um, a Copa Maori organization, um, that told us this.

Our kids are hearing on the news about funding cuts and have mentioned running away so they won't be kicked out of here. How do you manage those conversations when they're worried about the place closing? So I just, we need to always make sure we are listening to the voices of the kids and the impact that these decisions can have. And if you carry out a desk-based exercise without understanding the implications, these can be the consequences.

So thank you. Thank you. Uh, first question. He thank you. I actually have thousands of questions for you, so I'm gonna try and contain this one, but I would like to ask some very different questions. So I'll go right back on the list. Um, the first question I wanna ask is, one of the disturbing things I heard when I was talking to itself about this, and they didn't mean it to be, was they said that the organization is only for children in care.

And one of the things I saw in this report was, or, and, and the behavior that led to the report was, um, the cutting of funding to something like the family center up in Auckland, um, in North Shore. And that really works with where those children will be at risk if we don't do something about it.

You've just talked about that preventative work. Do you think that have a wide enough brief or are they narrowing it themselves because they're overwhelmed by the need out there? Um, okay. So I think, um, certainly at the time that was one of the questions we were asking to get clarity about the strategy.

And there were certainly mixed messages and, um, comments being made, um, about what the strategy was and what the focus was. So in particular where they really focusing in on their statutory responsibilities and care, um, at the expense of some of this early intervention prevention work. And so we've had reassurances that that's not the case, um, that they're continuing to fund those programs.

But I think the conversation needs to be had or is probably being had is what is Ang tamari's role? Are they there to focus on Tamari in care or even wider still those tamari that are known to them through points of concern that could be their focus area. And that's not to say that programs and services that focus on early intervention, those kids that are at risk of becoming known aren't important.

The challenges across all agency, who is going to pay for that? Who is responsible for those services to make sure that they continue? And one of the risks that we saw last year was that those conversations weren't necessarily happening, or tamari were potentially going to make decisions in a silo without having conversations across the sector about, well, if we step out of this, if we step out of this space, we're not saying the service doesn't have value, who can step in and pick it up and fund it.

And I think that remains a challenge. And as part of the conversation with the, um, establishment of, um, a social investment agency and what services they may fund from there, My follow up on this is I looked at, um, Australia and they have a focus on children, which is quite different. Do you think when you look at all the agencies that we've got in your own roles as well, we have enough actual clarity about the ownership of issues over the safety of children.

We have like terrible death rates. What's going wrong? I mean, in my view, uh, to that question, more needs to be done. The starting point for that is actually a collective, uh, decision in this country to prioritize children. Yes. To put the needs and the rights of central.

And first, uh, we know that if we, um, want to, uh, have a thriving, um, New Zealand into the future, it actually all starts with our children and our young people. And so investment in them from the very earliest years of their lives is absolutely crucial. Do we need to do more? Yes.

I think we need a more joined up approach across our children's system, as Aaron has spoken about. If that joined up approach had been taken in regard to the contracting and procurement process last year, I believe that, uh, things wouldn't have landed in where they did. I also think that it's crucial that we have a cross party commitment.

I knew that at our parliamentary, uh, system and level where actually we agree on some of the central tenets of what our child protection system needs to do, what it exists for and how it works. Yes. Uh, and I think we are still quite a long way from that. And so I urge you all across, um, parliament, It also costs money, doesn't it?

It, I mean, the Australians are spending $312 million on just that strategy on children. We just haven't put any money into it, have we? I think we have invested money in this area. Yeah. However, I think that, um, over we need to have a holistic view.

Um, if we look at our annual budget in this country, for example, that's why I really advocate for us to take a children's rights approach to our public budget so that we can join the dots and see the whole picture of the amount of investment that goes into children. And we need to see that across the children's system when it comes to our care and protection and youth justice system as well.

Thank you. I'll come To Laura next. Oh, thank you. Um, Mr. Chair and thank you guys for coming in this morning. Um, I've got a couple of questions. I guess my first one would be, um, around the orangutan who's handling of, um, previous procurement prior to this particular, um, incident.

We all know that this was not great, um, the way in which they've handled the contracts. Um, but from looking further back, it seems that they've always had a bit of a problem with contract management. And that's not just orangutan like MSD across this whole social network. And I've been hearing in certain communities about like, overlap of contracts, contracts not fit for purpose for delivery.

So people kind of manipulating them to get the outcomes for the community, um, working on outputs, not outcomes. Yeah. Um, would you be able to give me a bit of your reflection on that and how you think it would be best suited to be working in that space? Like what would you, you know, what would you expect them to look for in new contracts, for example?

And is this like actually more of a historic problem, not just, Yeah, I think that that's a fair comment in terms of being, um, consistent challenges with social service procurement. Um, one, one of the things is a particularly complicated area, and so you can contract a particular service. Um, but then to attribute, um, outcomes or outputs to that service can be a challenge because there are a lot of things that are impacting that family's life.

Mm-hmm. So at a simple level, you can quickly see an output. So did you visit the family? How many times have you visited? That's quite simple, straightforward. But if you then lift up to a success factor, which might be a reduction in notifications to Ang and attributing that to that service and basing that just on the, the data alone can be quite a challenge.

And then if you want to go to an outcome level and look at what the outcomes are, they've even further removed from that particular service. So there are real challenges with trying to get clarity over what is working. That's why it's really important that we consider both the, the stories and the experiences of the people who are both working and receiving the services and combining it with that data.

And that's the challenge. And that does take some time. Um, I think the other challenge is the maturity of the commissioning process. We've never got to the point of, um, the maturity where you could do it really well. And we talk about it. Um, and I think, um, orang, tamari may have alluded to it in their presentation, what you really want to do is have a really good understanding in a community or a region about the need.

Yep. And then you meet with the community and say, what are the services we need collectively to respond to those needs? Rather than dealing with providers as individuals and playing one off against the other. You do it in a collective way and you have a conversation now that requires the maturity of the organization, but also of those providers in the community to be willing to do it and work in that way.

And we certainly hear from 'em. They're willing to do it is a classic example. So when, um, funding cuts were made last year, um, got the providers together and said, don't talk to us individually about the changes. Talk to us as a collective. We all help you so solve this problem and tell you what needs to be done. And that's a much more mature way of operating and that's where we need to get to.

And then you need to give them time to deliver. So you then don't expect changes within a year. 'cause like I said, these are complex situations and they take time and you look, need to look at success by both the output at success factor and then ultimately an outcome level and combine all that with the stories of the experience. Because sometimes you just know the thing is working, even if you don't necessarily have the evidence.

Mm. Or you might, the the issue with it may be a problem with the referral process. So you can look at something and go, well, the referral rate isn't high. You can make a desk based exercise that that service isn't responding. But if you actually go and understand why the referral process isn't working, you get a different picture. And I think that's where the maturity level needs to be. It takes time and care to do it well.

And I don't think we've had the time and the space to do it. And that's what needs to happen. And that's what I mean by period of stability. Good. I really quickly, um, thank you for that answer. Do you think Arun Meki has like the systems, like the technology, the people, um, in order to get this procurement process, right? Because like we're talking about potentially a system that needs to be able to track a lot of information at one time.

Um, also talking to organizations on the ground, particularly the smaller ones, they really struggle with reporting and things like that because, you know, they're out there doing the money in the community and it's not really like their specialty niche to be, you know, filling in all kinds of different things. Um, so do you think that one, we've got those potentially those metrics, right?

I know you've talked about outcomes and outputs. I know there's heaps of outputs and honestly like some of 'em are ridiculous, um, that I've seen, you know, some of the organizations having to do and the outputs section of things. Um, but do you think that OT has that capability to be able to like actually do this correctly? Whether that is technology, um, whether that is personnel, Probably two points there. I think, um, I think you're right.

And you mentioned about providers having multiple contracts. They could have 17, 18 contracts they're administering and the cost involved in that is, is huge, right? It's an ft. Yeah. Um, that's right. And that's what we hear about. And I think, um, and then the challenge of that of course is they know what is actually needed. And so they use that money to deliver what they know is needed Mm.

As opposed to necessarily the the contract, um, specifications. And so this is why there's a, a, a, a requirement that you actually listen to the community about what is required and put that in place rather than potentially what you think might be needed from, from here in Wellington. Yeah. I think in terms of, is there the data and information available? No, not to the extent that people would like, I think there's a risk here.

Um, certainly a real good opportunity with the social investment agency to be able to do some of that stuff on behalf of, of agencies or behalf of regions and communities. That's a positive. I think there's a risk what's happened in Australia, but in terms of the demand for, um, uh, proving a return on investment for a particular service is it's grown an industry around how do we do this?

And that takes money and time again from providers and it's money and time they don't necessarily have because, um, contracts don't necessarily provide for that capability build to do that. And so there is a challenge, but I think you can still do best endeavors if you actually go out and listen and understand, um, what services are actually achieving and listen and understand from providers and then go with them, support them to do the work. Yeah.

Thank You. I wanted ask a supplementary on this, um, because I've asked a number of times, you know, interest is this outcomes versus outputs differentiation. And I think everyone agrees, yes, we need to focus on outcomes, but I think these are from, I've never really clearly articulated what good looks like mm-hmm. In terms of outcomes. And under underlying that is that for a very long time in New Zealand, we've had one of the worst rates of child abuse and child homicide in the developed world.

So we've got this, this longstanding structural challenge in New Zealand, and then we have the data and, and, and all these different organizations trying to do something that I, I don't feel if we've ever managed to get the, get adequate data and there's issues around privacy that they need to work through and, and, you know, sovereignty around date sovereignty and all this, how, how any thoughts on how we can better integrate this to address this very, very longstanding deep structural problem in New Zealand.

From my perspective, a key starting point there in terms of actually understanding what good outcomes look like is exactly what Aaron was speaking about, really focusing in on listening to the needs of children, young people, and whanau truly starting there, building on that understanding.

So it flips things, um, on the head. I guess a little bit in terms of the start point now is that a lot of these decisions tend to come from the center, so to speak, whereas actually we need to go to that local level and the regional level to truly understand what those needs are. I agree with you, Mr.

Chair, that there is a gap when it comes to truly comprehensive and integrated data around understanding the prevalence of the issues of child maltreatment in this country. That's why I advocate for New Zealand to undertake a child maltreatment study like Australia has over the past few years that has actually led to, um, their decision making around some of these approaches to addressing child maltreatment being much more evidence led and informed.

And I think we would do well to, uh, take that similar approach. And then if I can, I just want to pick up on, um, Ms. McClure's, um, earlier question. I want to acknowledge that, uh, she is right. These issues around orang, tamari contracting predate last year, and I had a had a close view into this, um, before coming into my current role as the former CEO of social service providers.

So if we go back to the 2022 contracting and procurement round, there were also significant, uh, issues at that time. I think last year we saw those go to an even, um, more significant, um, level. But for me, a couple of things really stand out. Uh, a lack of really solid and transparent communications between orang, tamari and its partners.

Um, a lack of true respect for the partnership that must exist in our child protection system between the Crown and Maori and the Crown and its wider NGO children's sector partners and also, um, a lack of holistic attention to understanding children's needs, both from a qualitative and a quantitative data perspective.

So these are issues that yes, have been longstanding. It is crucial that we now actually, um, see these recommendations of the Auditor General's report, um, implemented because if they are implemented, that will make a positive difference for children and out care and protection and youth justice system. Yeah. And I think in terms of the need for data and part of our role, and we see it in our reports, is to provide, um, yourselves with a view both in the quantitative and the story space and combining the two together so

that our recent outcomes from our report got some outcomes level information in it for you. And some of that information is new. So we, um, presented to the police here and they thanked us because actually some of the Youth justice information is the first time they'd seen data about these young people and the outcomes that they are, um, um, uh, uh, receiving.

Um, and that's really important because it helps to drive better decision making, but it's really important. We don't just focus on outcomes, but you need to have data points all along the way. So, like I said, the out point level success and then then outcome and you know, that, that there's that, um, data point that is crucial. I think the most crucial in that report was to understand that kids that actually offend, go on to offend, 93% of them have had care and protection concerns raised about them in the past.

And so there's your opportunity for an investment approach. If you can get it right at the earliest opportunity, then you might reduce the offending and reduce the number of victims of crime at the same time. That's your opportunity. And we will continue to measure this and continue to provide you with information so you can see if change is occurring, but we need to be patient because it will take time.

And I think this then speaks to Claire's point about having crossed party agreement on the strategy so that that doesn't deviate between election cycles. 'cause when that happens, it makes it even harder to achieve these outcomes. Thank you. Um, and thank you both here. Um, I'd like to go back list as well for another primary after this, and I have five supplementaries.

Um, the auditor general found that made contracting decisions in 4 25 without knowing how many tamari or whanau would be affected or whether services would remain accessible. And that's on page 47 and 48 of the OAG report. At the same time, we have seen a 34.5% increase in reports of concern affecting over 59,000 tam.

And that's on page three of the understanding reports of concern during 2025. In your view, what is driving the search? Is it cuts to the front line and back office staff or the failure to implement the kind of child-focused analysis? The auditor General Recomme?

Um, oh, did you want, okay. I was gonna say, um, we've, we've got a report coming out soon, um, which follows up on the Dan Carrum Pati review. And we are looking again at the reports of concern and how those are managed with, so there'll bit more information on that then. But I think in terms of, I first speak about the increase in reports of concern that we're seeing, I think it's due to a range of factors.

So, um, the Amman made a, um, decision about the way Ang tamari were recording contacts. So they were, um, treating, um, um, phone conversations as contact records for some children as opposed to treating them as reports of concern. So that's a practice change. And that will be partly due to, I think they recognize it's about at least a quarter of the increases just due to a change in reporting.

Um, so it's not an increase in actual reports a concern, I think you'll have a range of factors going in. One will be, um, from potentially a reduction in services and support for families, but also it'll be wider socioeconomic factors as well that are influencing, um, an increase. Um, and it's certainly an increase in reports of concern, but it's also an increase in the number of tamari that are known, although that's not quite as high, um, as the reports are concerned.

So within it, you're seeing re notifications and this is what I mean by about investing at the earliest opportunity and a measure for success. If you can get a service in place for the whanau that then prevents a further reification, reduces the burden on order a tamari and keeps kids safer. So it's all of those things that are playing out within it. Mm-hmm. And I agree, it's absolutely multifaceted.

We can't point to one particular thing. Our child protection system has been under pressure for many years, um, before 2024. Yes. We know that there have been added pressures on that system. We've seen, um, a restructure to ang Tamari. We have seen the impacts of these funding and contracting decisions.

But we do need to couch that in a broader context. And as Aaron said, um, the many socioeconomic factors that impact directly on children's lives and on whanau lives. Um, we know that the cost of living crisis has affected everyone in this country, but especially for our low income families and whanau, the impacts continue to be huge.

And we only have to look at this year's child poverty reduction statistics. Yeah. To see that we continue to track in the wrong direction. Um, I want to see us truly commit to addressing child poverty in this country. Um, because in a small, wealthy nation as we are relatively speaking, we simply shouldn't have such high numbers of children, uh, living in poverty.

And whilst poverty is not determinative of, um, children being maltreated, we do know that poverty places huge stresses on families in Hanau. And so if we go to the prevention discussion that we are having mm-hmm. That's why we must join the dots here and actually recognize that, um, you know, that's where we need to pay attention.

Also, I really want us to not forget the long tail impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic and the ripple effects that it continues to have in the lives of children and their families in. Thank you. Um, so how concerned are you that the forecast 182 million in further cuts over the next four years will worsen Ang Tam's ability to respond effectively if reports of concern?

I think, um, the challenge again is report concern is made and then an assessment's made that something needs to be done either for the w or for that young person. Right. And it's then the need to actually have those services available. And so if those are being reduced and it places, um, pressure not only on Ang Tamari staff to do what they know is needed, but then it also places, um, challenges on the whanau and the young person, which can play out into, like I just described before, further involvement in the system.

And I'll give you a, a quote from, um, Ang Tamari staff themselves. And this comes from the Bay of Plenty following the contract, um, reduction. So we no longer have a list of providers in toga who have had their contract renewed. We are making referrals and hoping for the best. And this is where they're talking about local supported bail providers.

Yep. So we now have a 12 week waiting list, and it's likely to increase, um, in the email it said it was because Ang he cut our contracts, we lean on them. This is a provider speaking and have done a fabulous job for whanau and working with kids in hiring complex needs. So this is where the services just aren't available. And this could be mentors or supported bail.

And so the, and what the implication of that is, is I've got another quote from here is that they're left, they're then left to support that young person. And then they described because they couldn't keep them occupied, they then went on to offend. 'cause the services weren't in place to support them. And once they offend, suddenly the services get put in place.

And so the point is it shouldn't take someone to offend before the system can actually respond and provide the needs. Those were needed in this at the time that they were on subjected bail. And this is the challenge of services continue to get cut. And what really concerns me is what we see laid out in Ang Tam's, recent reports of concern report, where number of those measures it is saying that it is going to be significantly under pressure to be able to respond.

Its ability to respond to the volume modeled, volume predicted of reports of concern. Mm-hmm. And so what I really want to see there is a whole of children's system response that recognizes that, as I said publicly when that report was published, this is Ang tam itself sounding the alarm about the pressure on our child protection system.

And actually the whole of the children's system needs to come together and respond with urgency, um, so that we don't end up in a situation where our child protection system goes under that pressure that is being predicted. I'll just ask one more. Yeah. I said five, but I was so at three. What if you waiting? Yeah. Um, so should independent child impact assessments be required before any further contracting or budget decisions?

In my view, a core aspect of Orang Tamari's decision making actually in relation to all its decision making, but including in relation to its procurement and contracting decision making is to have a clear approach to, uh, understanding the impact of its decisions on the children that will be affected by those decisions.

And that is at both ends of decision making. So decision making in relation to, um, putting in place new contracts, for example, and new services and, um, procuring new services as well as decisions to deinvest or decommission services. Because absolutely over time in our social sector, more widely decisions will be taken to decommission or divest in particular services.

But my concern when it comes to children is how their needs and their rights are taken into account in that decision making process. Mm-hmm. And I think for me, the, the statement in the auditor general's report that stood out to me so starkly was this one, we did not see evidence that understood how its decisions would affect children and their families.

So order Tamari and our wider children's system that contracts services that impact on children need to really pay attention to that finding of the auditor general's report and actually learn from that and put in place systems and processes that have a clear and transparent approach to understanding children's needs and factor factoring their needs and rights into those decisions.

We've got. Maureen, uh, thank you Mr. Chair. Um, it feels like a big question, but we talk about ot, which by very existence is the ambulance at the bottom of the cliff. So if we were to look at the fence at the top, what are the things that miss are missing that, that have so many children born into poverty?

You know, should we be talking more about contraception at that point? Or why is it that we have so many parents who are struggling to be good parents? You know, where is the education need at the top of the cliff? Um, it just seems that we're, we are fighting, it's like lemmings falling off the cliff.

You know, we're fighting that always at the bottom. What can we do Yeah. To actually prevent it? Um, that's a good point. I was gonna add that onto what Bruce said.
